reasoning: The FRED series SNDR has exhibited a clear downward trend over the past year, dropping from 0.46 in August 2024 to 0.39 in August 2025. Although there was a minor positive bounce from 0.38 in July to 0.39 in August, the macroeconomic environment points overwhelmingly toward the Fed either holding rates flat or cutting them through the rest of 2025 and 2026 (some analysts forecast up to 7 cuts in 2026). Consequently, the core expectation is that the national savings rate will remain stable or drift downward, making a strict increase (>0.39) unlikely. For the short-term September resolution, the probability is set very low at 18%, anchoring on the FRED statistical trend model and current downward momentum. For more distant horizons (November 2025 and February 2026), predicting exact economic conditions carries significantly higher variance. While the most likely trajectory is still downward due to anticipated Fed dovishness, the right tail of the expectation distribution naturally widens due to unresolved macroeconomic uncertainties (e.g., potential policy shifts or inflation resurgence prompting hikes). Thus, adhering to the principle that uncertainty should increase with the forecast horizon, the probabilities progressively rise toward 0.5 (28% for November, 38% for February).
